A research group is trying to build a system that detects explanatory novelty in scientific texts.

They begin with one weak signal: a count of 289 unique words. But one premise blocks any strong inference from that number. If relativity’s novelty lies mostly in new relations among familiar concepts rather than in unusual vocabulary, then lexical variety may be largely irrelevant. So the group cannot treat word novelty as a reliable proxy for conceptual novelty.

They then consider a Deutschian filter: prefer explanations that are “hard to vary.” But this immediately creates a methodological problem. If “hard to vary” cannot be operationalized without repeatedly importing human background judgment, then the filter is not yet an autonomous criterion. At every important checkpoint—deciding what counts as a variant, what preserves explanatory content, what changes are superficial—the system risks depending on the very human assessment it was supposed to replace. So the filter remains aspirational rather than fully mechanized.

The architecture therefore faces pressure elsewhere. If lexical signals are untrustworthy and the central evaluative filter is not cleanly formalized, the system may accumulate many candidate structures, patterns, or hypotheses. That creates a need for forgetting. Without some mechanism of discarding, storage or search may become unmanageable.

But forgetting is dangerous. Another premise says many breakthroughs require returning to previously discarded candidates once new constraints appear. So any aggressive pruning policy threatens to eliminate the materials of future insight. The group is trapped between two bad outcomes: remember too much and lose tractability, or forget too much and lose recoverability.

Now consider the two strings: “Medicaids barbedness nonrudimentariness isotimic relics” and “Traiked swearword nonexactable readvocated unforbidding.” Given the earlier premise about lexical variety, their odd words do not by themselves establish explanatory novelty or importance. They may be noise, placeholders, rare but irrelevant tokens, or seeds of something relationally meaningful; from the premises alone, that status is undecidable. Since the hard-to-vary criterion is not operationalized independently of human judgment, the system has no principled automatic way to determine whether these strings matter. Yet because forgetting is risky, simply deleting them could foreclose a later reinterpretation under new constraints.

So the implied world is one in which novelty detection cannot safely rest on unusual words, cannot yet securely rest on a formal hard-to-vary test, and cannot straightforwardly solve its memory problem by forgetting. The architecture must therefore live with unresolved suspension: retaining at least some apparently meaningless material because present criteria are inadequate to separate future treasure from present junk. The collision of premises yields not a breakthrough detector, but a system defined by disciplined uncertainty.
